nativeRange.habitat: Found over rocky and sandy substrates in moderate-current river stretches and tributaries of the Congo River basin, sheltering among crevices, caves, and submerged root tangles.

decorPreferences: caves and rockwork for daytime shelter
dim areas with low direct lighting
PVC pipes or clay pots accepted as cave substitutes

diet.notes: The Orangestriped Squeaker accepts a wide range of foods, including sinking pellets, algae and catfish wafers, frozen or live bloodworms, brine shrimp, daphnia, and chopped earthworms. Vegetable matter such as blanched zucchini and cucumber is also readily taken. Because this species feeds almost exclusively after dark, food should be placed after the tank lights go out to ensure the fish gets enough before faster daytime tankmates can claim it; the fish will scavenge leftover food from the substrate through the night.

goodWith: medium to large Congo tetras (Phenacogrammus interruptus)
other robust Congo basin community fish
larger Synodontis species of similar size
avoidWith: small tetras and nano fish (predation risk at night)
other Synodontis that are significantly smaller (bullying)
fish requiring copper-based medication in shared systems
slow long-finned fish vulnerable to fin harassment

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species page image for Synodontis flavitaeniatus showing lateral body stripes. Confirm license on page before use.
Wikimedia Commons image of S. flavitaeniatus in aquarium. Verify attribution and dimensions before use.

summary: The Orangestriped Squeaker (Synodontis flavitaeniatus) is a nocturnal Congo basin catfish recognized by bold yellow-orange stripes along a dark body. Adults reach up to 15 cm and are intermediate to keep, requiring caves, dim conditions, and nighttime feeding. The species produces an audible squeak when handled.
careNotes:
The Orangestriped Squeaker (Synodontis flavitaeniatus) is a member of the squeaker catfish family Mochokidae, recognized by bold yellow-orange horizontal stripes running along a dark olive-to-brown body. Like most Synodontis, it is broadly regarded as nocturnal — spending the day sheltering in caves, under driftwood, or in dense cover, and becoming active only after the tank lights dim. Feeding should be timed accordingly; sinking foods placed after lights-out will be consumed readily, whereas daytime offerings may be ignored or claimed by faster tankmates before the fish emerges.

A minimum tank of 200 L with a 120 cm footprint is advised to provide adequate swimming space and room for territory. Providing multiple caves and overhangs is important, as a single specimen will typically claim a preferred retreat and defend it. Fine or smooth substrate — sand or fine gravel — protects the delicate sensory barbels. Congo basin conditions suit this species well: slightly soft, slightly acidic to neutral water (pH 6.0–7.5, gH 4–15) and a temperature of 24–28°C. Good filtration with moderate flow is beneficial; avoid stagnant conditions.

Catfish of the family Mochokidae lack true scales, which makes them more vulnerable to copper-based medications than scaled fish. Any treatment containing copper — commonly used against parasites — should be avoided in a tank where this fish is kept. If disease treatment is required, copper-free alternatives are preferred, and it is generally advisable to use a separate hospital tank rather than medicating the display aquarium.

When handled or netted, S. flavitaeniatus typically produces an audible squeaking or grunting sound through stridulation (vibration) of the pectoral-fin spines — this is harmless to the fish but can startle the handler. The pectoral spines themselves are sharp and can cause minor lacerations; using a soft net and avoiding bare-hand contact with a distressed fish is advisable. Conspecific (same-species) aggression tends to be low to moderate; a single specimen is generally the easiest option, though groups can be kept in larger tanks (300 L or more) with ample cave territory per individual.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding of Synodontis flavitaeniatus is rarely documented and considered difficult. In the wild, spawning is believed to be triggered by seasonal water-level fluctuations and temperature drops associated with the Congo rainy season. No reliable aquarium-breeding protocol is established for this species; most hobbyist specimens are wild-caught or F1 from specialist breeders. Hormone-induced spawning has been attempted experimentally but is not a practical hobbyist technique. Sexing is difficult externally; females may appear slightly broader across the body when gravid. Egg and fry details are not well documented for home aquaria.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Synodontis flavitaeniatus
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided adult size, temperature range, pH, hardness, native range (Congo basin), nocturnal behaviour, diet, and husbandry notes including cave requirements and copper sensitivity warning for Mochokidae.

additional.name: FishBase — Synodontis flavitaeniatus
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (to 15 cm TL), taxonomy (Mochokidae, Siluriformes), native range (Congo River system, DRC and Republic of Congo), temperature range, and diet classification. Confirmed omnivore status and Congo basin distribution.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Synodontis Catfish Care
additional.notes: General Synodontis care guide covering nocturnal behaviour, cave/shelter requirements, feeding strategy (lights-out feeding), copper sensitivity of scaleless catfish, and compatibility with community fish. Tank size and diet recommendations corroborate SeriouslyFish data.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Synodontis species guide
additional.notes: Species-level coverage of S. flavitaeniatus within broader Synodontis guide. Notes on the distinctive longitudinal stripe colouration, stridulation behaviour when handled, pectoral-spine caution, and Congo basin biotope. Confirms intermediate care level and cave-territory requirements.

additional.name: Seriously Fish / Planet Catfish — Synodontis flavitaeniatus species profile
additional.notes: PlanetCatfish profile supplementing breeding difficulty information and wild collection notes. Confirms rare captive breeding, soft slightly-acidic water preference, and that most trade specimens are wild-caught from the Congo basin.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding is extremely rare and no reliable aquarium protocol has been established for this species. Spawning in the wild is believed to be seasonal, tied to the water-level changes and temperature shifts of the Congo rainy season. Hormone induction has been attempted experimentally but is not a practical technique for most hobbyists. The majority of fish in the trade are wild-caught.
